behaviors.description: Trace ID grouping correctly clusters entries from different microservices
behaviors.justification: Grouping is pure deterministic logic on structured data. Given a set of log entries with known trace IDs from different sources, the output groups are deterministic. No I/O, concurrency, or external dependencies; a direct function call with test data is sufficient to catch grouping logic failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create test log entries from multiple distinct microservices (e.g., auth-service, payment-service, order-service) each emitting 3–5 entries with the same trace ID, plus entries with different trace IDs. Call the grouping function and verify: (a) all entries sharing a trace ID are in the same group, (b) entries with different trace IDs are in separate groups, (c) group count equals number of unique trace IDs.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit-level test directly exercises the trace ID grouping logic with structured test data; no mocking or infrastructure needed.

behaviors.description: Timestamp ordering correctly sorts entries despite up to ±500ms clock skew between services
behaviors.justification: Ordering is deterministic logic applied to timestamps. Given a set of entries with known timestamps (some offset by clock skew), the sort order is always the same. Clock skew is just a data characteristic; the ordering algorithm either handles it correctly or doesn't. No I/O or concurrency needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create log entries with timestamps ranging from −500ms to +500ms relative to a reference timestamp, simulating clock skew across services. Test edge cases: entries at exactly ±500ms skew, ±499ms, ±501ms. Call the timeline ordering function and verify entries are sorted in ascending timestamp order. Verify that entries differing by 500ms are ordered correctly regardless of emission sequence.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test with deterministic timestamp inputs verifies the sort algorithm handles clock skew correctly; no external timing needed.

behaviors.description: Latency spikes (p99 exceeding 3× baseline) are detected and flagged in anomaly report
behaviors.justification: Spike detection is a threshold-based algorithm on latency statistics. We can test the algorithm directly with synthetic latency data where we control the baseline and spike values, making p99 deterministic and testable. The failure mode (wrong threshold, missing spike, false positive) is caught by unit-level logic testing.
behaviors.test_description: Create synthetic latency distributions: (a) baseline with mean ~100ms, p99 ~150ms; (b) perturbed to p99 = 450ms (exactly 3× baseline); (c) perturbed to p99 = 449ms (just under 3×); (d) perturbed to p99 = 451ms (just over 3×). Pass each to the spike detection function and verify: baseline and 449ms cases do not flag, 450ms+ cases do flag spike as anomaly.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test directly tests the spike detection algorithm with known baseline and p99 values; no need for real request traces flowing through the system for the core logic test.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline; skip count is reported accurately
behaviors.justification: Error handling for JSON parsing is testable at the parser boundary with real (not mocked) JSON input. The failure mode is: (a) crash on bad input, (b) wrong skip count. A unit test passing real malformed JSON strings to the parser catches both. No mocking of json.parse() or file I/O layers needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test dataset with a mix of valid and intentionally malformed JSON entries: missing closing braces, invalid escape sequences, truncated JSON, mismatched quotes, invalid numeric types (e.g., NaN), missing required fields. Pass the entire dataset to the parser in sequence. Verify: (a) parser does not crash or throw uncaught exception, (b) skip counter increments exactly once per malformed entry, (c) valid entries in the dataset are still processed correctly, (d) test with at least 50 entries including 15+ distinct malformed variants.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test uses real malformed JSON strings (no mocks); tests the parser's error-handling logic at the input boundary.

behaviors.description: Tool processes 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind queue
behaviors.justification: This is a throughput and concurrency test. A unit test of a single log parser function will not expose queue overflow, unbounded backlog growth, race conditions, or thread synchronization bottlenecks—failures that only manifest under real concurrent load with actual timing. We need multiple producers competing for pipeline resources, real queue behavior, and measurements over sustained load.
behaviors.test_description: Set up an integration test harness with: (a) multiple concurrent producer threads/async tasks emitting log entries at a combined rate of 10,000 entries/sec (e.g., 4 producers at 2,500 each), (b) the actual ingestion pipeline consuming from a shared queue, (c) measurement over 10+ seconds of sustained load. Measure: total entries processed, count of dropped entries, queue depth over time. Verify: 100% of emitted entries are successfully ingested (zero drops), queue depth remains bounded (does not grow unboundedly), and processing latency does not degrade significantly over the test duration.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration test required because queue overflow, backlog growth, and synchronization issues only emerge under concurrent load with real timing; unit test of parser alone would miss these failures.
